sentences: The Maine Red Claws are a professional minor league basketball team based in Portland, Maine.
The Red Claws compete in the NBA G League and are affiliated with the Boston Celtics.
The team played its first season in 2009–10.
The team's home court is the Portland Expo Building.
The team is owned by Maine Basketball, LLC, which is chaired by William Ryan, Jr.
The Red Claws made their first playoff appearance on April 6, 2013, as the eighth seeded team and was swept by the Rio Grande Valley Vipers in the opening round.
The NBA G League All-Star Game is an annual exhibition basketball game held by the NBA G League.
The G League was founded in 2001 as the National Basketball Development League (NBDL) and later as the NBA Development League (D-League).
The league adopted its current name at the start of the 2017–18 season.
The league serves as the National Basketball Association's official minor league basketball organization.
Raptors 905 are a Canadian professional basketball team in the NBA G League.
The team is based in Mississauga, Ontario, and began play in the 2015–16 season.

The club, which is the G League affiliate of the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association, plays their home games at the Hershey Centre, replacing the Centre's former basketball tenant, the Mississauga Power of the National Basketball League of Canada.
The team also plays occasional home games at the Air Canada Centre, the home of their parent club.
Raptors 905 were the eighth D-League team to be owned by an NBA team and the first to be based outside the United States.
The Washington NBA G League team is an American professional basketball team in the NBA G League and an affiliate of the Washington Wizards of the National Basketball Association.
Based in Washington, D.C., the team will play their home games during the 2018–19 season at the St. Elizabeths East Entertainment and Sports Arena.
The team became the twenty-third G League team to be owned by an NBA team.
The NBA G League is the National Basketball Association's official minor league basketball organization.
The league was known as the National Basketball Development League (NBDL) from 2001 to 2005, and the NBA Development League (NBA D-League) from 2005 until 2017.

The league started with eight teams until NBA commissioner David Stern announced a plan to expand the NBA D-League to fifteen teams and develop it into a true minor league farm system, with each NBA D-League team affiliated with one or more NBA teams in March 2005.
At the conclusion of the 2013–14 NBA season, 33% of NBA players had spent time in the NBA D-League, up from 23% in 2011.
As of the 2017–18 season, the league consists of 26 teams, all of which are either single-affiliated or owned by an NBA team.
These are regular season standings and playoff results for the NBA G League.
The NBA G League is the official minor league basketball organization owned and run by the National Basketball Association (NBA).
The league was formed in 2001 as the National Basketball Development League (NBDL).
The league was renamed to NBA Development League (NBA D-League) in 2005 to reflect its close affiliation with the NBA.
In 2017, it was renamed NBA G League, as part of a sponsorship deal with Gatorade.
Edward J. "E. J." Singler (born June 6, 1990) is an American professional basketball player for Raptors 905 of the NBA G League.
He played college basketball for the University of Oregon.
As a senior at Oregon, he was named to the All-Pac-12 first team.
Kyle Edward Singler (born May 4, 1988) is an American professional basketball player for the Oklahoma City Thunder of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
Singler was a four-year starter for the Duke men's basketball team and was instrumental in their 2010 NCAA championship run, earning MVP of the Final Four.
In the 2011 NBA draft, the Detroit Pistons selected Singler in the second round with the overall 33rd pick.
Singler joined the Pistons for the 2012–13 season after playing in Spain during 2011–12.
The Iowa Wolves are an American professional basketball team based in Des Moines, Iowa.
It is owned by and affiliated with the Minnesota Timberwolves as of the 2017–18 season.
They play in the Western Conference in the NBA G League, a minor league basketball organization run by the National Basketball Association (NBA).
The Wolves play their home games at the Wells Fargo Arena.
From 2007 to 2017, the team was known as the Iowa Energy in the NBA Development League (D-League) until being purchased and renamed by the Timberwolves.
They broke the D-league attendance record on their first ever home game with 8,842 fans.
They later set the record again in game two of the 2011 D-League Finals with an attendance of 14,036 fans.
They won the 2011 D-League Finals, defeating the Rio Grande Valley Vipers two-games-to-one.
The Fort Wayne Mad Ants are a team of the NBA G League, located in Fort Wayne, Indiana.
The Allen County War Memorial Coliseum is the home venue for the team.
The team is the first minor league basketball franchise to play in Fort Wayne since the Fort Wayne Fury of the Continental Basketball Association.
The Mad Ants won their first D-League championship in 2014.
In September 2015, the Indiana Pacers purchased the Mad Ants, thus becoming the Pacers' one-to-one D-League affiliate.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: E. J. Singler plays for the Canadian G League basketball team based in what city?
hop1_docs: Kyle Singler | Kyle Edward Singler (born May 4, 1988) is an American professional basketball player for the Oklahoma City Thunder of the National Basketball Association (NBA).  Singler was a four-year starter for the Duke men's basketball team and was instrumental in their 2010 NCAA championship run, earning MVP of the Final Four.  In the 2011 NBA draft, the Detroit Pistons selected Singler in the second round with the overall 33rd pick.  Singler joined the Pistons for the 2012–13 season after playing in Spain during 2011–12.

E. J. Singler | Edward J. "E. J." Singler (born June 6, 1990) is an American professional basketball player for Raptors 905 of the NBA G League.  He played college basketball for the University of Oregon.  As a senior at Oregon, he was named to the All-Pac-12 first team.

Sherri Singler | Sherri Nadine Singler (born February 19, 1974, in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan as Sherri Leonard) is a Canadian curler from Harris, Saskatchewan.  She currently plays second for the Stefanie Lawton team.

Saigon Heat | The Saigon Heat (Vietnamese: "Sài Gòn Heat" ) are a Vietnamese professional basketball team based in Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam.  They play in the ASEAN Basketball League.  Since the 2014 season, the Heat's home games are played at the CIS Arena on the campus of the Canadian International School in District 7.  Saigon Heat is the first professional basketball team based in Vietnam.

Raptors 905 | Raptors 905 are a Canadian professional basketball team in the NBA G League.  The team is based in Mississauga, Ontario, and began play in the 2015–16 season.  The club, which is the G League affiliate of the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association, plays their home games at the Hershey Centre, replacing the Centre's former basketball tenant, the Mississauga Power of the National Basketball League of Canada.  The team also plays occasional home games at the Air Canada Centre, the home of their parent club.  Raptors 905 were the eighth D-League team to be owned by an NBA team and the first to be based outside the United States.

Mississauga Power | The Mississauga Power were a Canadian professional basketball team based in Mississauga, Ontario, that competed in the National Basketball League of Canada (NBL).  Established in as the Oshawa Power, they played in the Central Division.  The Oshawa team began play in the inaugural NBL Canada season, along with the London Lightning, Moncton Miracles, and Summerside Storm and three Premier Basketball League (PBL) teams.

Saint John Riptide | The Saint John Riptide are a Canadian professional basketball team based in Saint John, New Brunswick.  They compete in the National Basketball League of Canada (NBL Canada) as a member of the Atlantic Division.  The Riptide play their home games at Harbour Station.  The Riptide are one of two professional basketball teams from the province of New Brunswick, the other being the Moncton Miracles.  The team was established in 2007 as the Manchester Millrats, an expansion team based in Manchester, New Hampshire, which played in the American Basketball Association (ABA).  They would later move to the Premier Basketball League (PBL) in 2008, relocated to Saint John in 2010, and renamed in 2016.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- E. J. Singler — [2] — "is an American professional basketball player for Raptors 905 of the NBA G League."
- Raptors 905 — [5] — "The team is based in Mississauga, Ontario, and began play in the 2015–16 season."

Ambiguities:
- None found; "E. J. Singler" maps to the single passage [2], and "Raptors 905" maps to passage [5].

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing relation; the passages explicitly link E. J. Singler to Raptors 905 and state the team's city.

Retrieval direction:
- Page: "Raptors 905"
- Page: "E. J. Singler"
- Query: "Raptors 905 based in Mississauga Ontario"
Final conclusion: Mississauga, Ontario.
hop2_docs: Raptors 905 | Raptors 905 are a Canadian professional basketball team in the NBA G League.  The team is based in Mississauga, Ontario, and began play in the 2015–16 season.  The club, which is the G League affiliate of the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association, plays their home games at the Hershey Centre, replacing the Centre's former basketball tenant, the Mississauga Power of the National Basketball League of Canada.  The team also plays occasional home games at the Air Canada Centre, the home of their parent club.  Raptors 905 were the eighth D-League team to be owned by an NBA team and the first to be based outside the United States.

2014–15 Mississauga Power season | The 2014–15 Mississauga Power season was the fourth and final season of the franchise in the National Basketball League of Canada (NBL).  It was also their second season in Mississauga.  Following an unsuccessful season with a record of 7–25, the Power folded, resulting in the creation of Raptors 905, a new NBA Development League affiliate to the NBA's Toronto Raptors.

Lorenzo Brown | Lorenzo Brown (born August 26, 1990) is an American professional basketball player for the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association (NBA), on a two-way contract with the Raptors' NBA G League affiliate, the Raptors 905.  He played college basketball for North Carolina State University.

Malcolm Miller (basketball) | Malcolm Miller (born March 6, 1993) is an American professional basketball player for the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association (NBA).  He was signed on a two-way contract with the Raptors' NBA G League affiliate Raptors 905.  He played college basketball for Holy Cross.

Air Canada Centre | The Air Canada Centre (ACC) is a multi-purpose indoor sporting arena located on Bay Street in the South Core district of Downtown Toronto, Ontario, Canada.  It is the home of the Toronto Maple Leafs of the National Hockey League (NHL), the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association (NBA) and the Toronto Rock of the National Lacrosse League (NLL).  In addition, the Toronto Marlies of the American Hockey League play occasional games at the arena, and the Raptors 905 of the NBA G League play occasional games at the venue as well.  The area was previously home to the Toronto Phantoms of the Arena Football League (AFL) during their brief existence.  The arena is popularly known as "the ACC".

Mississauga Trilliums | The Mississauga Trilliums are a girls lacrosse association based in Mississauga, Ontario, Canada.  The Trilliums are associated with the Mississauga Tomahawks in the Ontario Lacrosse Association and were founded in 2006.  The Trilliums play in the "Ontario Women's Lacrosse" league.

Mississauga—Malton | Mississauga—Malton is a federal electoral district in Ontario.  It encompasses a portion of Ontario previously included in the electoral districts of Bramalea—Gore—Malton, Mississauga—Brampton South and Mississauga—Streetsville.
response.summary_2: E. J. Singler plays for Raptors 905, and Raptors 905 are based in Mississauga, Ontario.
